coredata.dc:description: Hybrid supercapacitors (HSCs) with large energy and power density have received increasing concern for their great potential in satisfying the requirements of energy storage systems. Nonetheless, the imbalanced kinetics between the sluggish battery-type cathode and the rapid capacitor-type anode make it difficult for HSCs to achieve high energy density at high power density. Herein, a novel N-doped carbon quantum dots/Ni-Co-Se (N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se) hollow microspheres composite is synthesized via a facile hydrothermal approach using bifunctional CQDs as size regulators and conductive agents for the first time. Thanks to the synergism of highly conductive N-CQDs with rapid electron transfer and reduced-size hollow micro-/nanostructures contributing to the enhanced ion transport, the as-prepared battery-type N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres composite cathode exhibits admirable rate property. In-depth electrochemical kinetic analyses and density functional theory (DFT) calculations are utilized to elucidate the preeminent kinetic properties. Furthermore, a novel HSC is fabricated based on the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres composite cathode with ultrafast electrochemical kinetics, displaying a high energy density of 23.1 Wh kg−1 at a superb power density of 38.3 kW kg−1. This encouraging work provides a good strategy to construct ultrahigh rate battery-type electrode materials with tunable size and component for simultaneously obtaining high energy/power density HSCs.

head.ce:title: Battery-type Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres cathode materials enabled by bifunctional N-doped carbon quantum dots with ultrafast electrochemical kinetics for hybrid supercapacitors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The bifunctional N-CQDs are used as size regulators and conductive agents to synthesize novel N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres composite, exhibiting ultrahigh rate performance, which is deeply explored by electrochemical kinetic analyses and DFT calculations. Further, the hybrid supercapacitor based on N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres composite cathode delivers a high energy density (23.1 Wh kg−1) at a superb power density (38.3 kW kg−1).

Hybrid supercapacitors (HSCs) with large energy and power density have received increasing concern for their great potential in satisfying the requirements of energy storage systems. Nonetheless, the imbalanced kinetics between the sluggish battery-type cathode and the rapid capacitor-type anode make it difficult for HSCs to achieve high energy density at high power density. Herein, a novel N-doped carbon quantum dots/Ni-Co-Se (N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se) hollow microspheres composite is synthesized via a facile hydrothermal approach using bifunctional CQDs as size regulators and conductive agents for the first time. Thanks to the synergism of highly conductive N-CQDs with rapid electron transfer and reduced-size hollow micro-/nanostructures contributing to the enhanced ion transport, the as-prepared battery-type N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres composite cathode exhibits admirable rate property. In-depth electrochemical kinetic analyses and density functional theory (DFT) calculations are utilized to elucidate the preeminent kinetic properties. Furthermore, a novel HSC is fabricated based on the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres composite cathode with ultrafast electrochemical kinetics, displaying a high energy density of 23.1 Wh kg−1 at a superb power density of 38.3 kW kg−1. This encouraging work provides a good strategy to construct ultrahigh rate battery-type electrode materials with tunable size and component for simultaneously obtaining high energy/power density HSCs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The sharp decline of fossil fuels and a worsening ecological environment have brought researchers to focus on environment-friendly and renewable energy sources in the midst of rapid worldwide economic growth and population expansion. In recent years, enormous efforts have been tried to exploit reproducible energy sources, including solar, wind and tidal energy. However, an uneven distribution of these renewable energy sources in terms of seasonal, geographical and temporal dimensions has greatly limited their direct use in daily life and industry. Therefore, green, efficient, low-cost and high-property energy storage devices are particularly crucial for energy harvesting and conversion [1–5]. Among popular candidates for electrochemical energy storage systems, supercapacitors (SCs) and batteries have received enormous attention. SCs generally represent the merits of high power performance, rapid charging and preeminent cycle stability despite relatively low energy density, while batteries generally exhibit superior energy density features despite inferior power performance. Hybrid supercapacitors (HSCs) have the capability of combining the advantages of SCs and batteries without attenuating the power density and cycle stability of SCs to obtain a high energy density at the same time. Thus, HSCs are conceived as one of the most hopeful next generation energy storage systems [6–8].

As we all know, the electrode materials as the crucial components of HSCs directly determine the performance of HSCs. Among various electrode materials, battery-type electrode materials with multiple oxidation states are widely researched because they can provide rich redox reactions to obtain high specific capacity [9–12]. Battery-type electrode materials typically include transition metal oxides (TMOs), transition metal hydroxide and transition metal sulfides (TMSs). Unfortunately, their inherent defects of low specific capacity and inferior electronic conductivity have limited the practical application. In contrast, transition metal selenides (TMSes) are more suitable high-performance electroactive materials than TMOs and TMSs due to the low bandgap, multiple valence and excellent electrical conductivity [13,14]. Moreover, TMSes exhibit better cycle stability since they do not generate polyselenide intermediates during the charging-discharging reaction [15]. Of particular note is that the co-presence of bimetallic electroactive centers will provide more abundant multi-electron redox reactions than the corresponding monocomponent selenium and properly present the inherent interactions and/or synergies between the two [16]. In addition, bimetallic selenides have been verified to exhibit better electrochemical properties than those of monometallic selenides due to the synergistic interaction of diverse metal ions [17]. It is worth noting that the electrochemical performance of single nickel selenide or cobalt selenide cannot be compared with that of binary nickel–cobalt selenide because the coupling of Ni and Co is more helpful in regulating electronic structure and enhancing electrical conductivity [18,19]. Despite of these merits, the rate performance and cycling stability of binary Ni-Co selenides still require further improvement.

To overcome these drawbacks, various carbon materials, such as carbon fibers [20], graphene [21] and carbon nanotubes [22] have been employed to prepare composites to improve the electrochemical properties of TMSes. Among various carbon-based materials available, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a kind of zero-dimensional materials with a size below 10 nm, have been widely employed in diverse fields such as biomolecular analysis, biosensing, photocatalysis and solar cell resulting from their excellent properties of biocompatibility, nontoxicity, low cost and good electronic conductivity [23,24]. Recently, CQDs have gained increasing attention in energy storage as a type of potential candidate electrode material for SCs [25]. Compared with traditional carbon materials, CQDs possess abundant edge positions and surface functional groups, which have strong adsorption ability for electrolyte ions and provide abundant active sites for enhancing electrochemical performance. Additionally, CQDs can enable the fabrication of various structures with more flexibility than multidimensional carbon materials. It is worth noting that hetero atoms (N, B, S, P) doping, especially N atoms, can induce structural and electronic distortions in carbon materials, including Fermi levels, charge transport and local electronic states [26,27]. Therefore, the N-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) can exhibit enhanced electronic conductivity and wettability, which is instrumental in boosting the electrochemical properties of the composites. In our series of pioneering works [28–30], CQDs were utilized as intercalators (or structure-directing agents) and conductive agents to construct composites, such as N-CQDs/rGO/Fe2O3 [28], CQDs/NiCo2O4 [29] and N,S-CQDs/NiCo2S4 [30], achieving highly desirable electrochemical properties. When CQDs were used as structure-directing agents and conductive agents, they could regulate the structure of electrode materials and effectively boost the conductivity of the composites, thereby contributing to improved electrochemical properties. Additionally, CQDs as intercalators also could alleviate the agglomeration of electrode materials and expand the specific surface area (SSA) of the composites, thus effectively boosting the energy storage performance of the composites. However, there are few reports on CQDs used both as size regulators and conductive agents to construct composites. Combining the above advantages, the multifunctional CQDs can provide special advantages for improving the electrochemical energy storage performance of TMSes. Thus, it is hopeful that the TMSes modified by N-CQDs will exhibit excellent electrochemical energy storage performance.

Besides, the rational structure design is considered as another viable approach to optimize the electrochemical performance of TMSes. Recently, hollow micro-/nanostructures have attracted extensive attention on account of their compelling structural features such as great SSA, low density, short diffusion route and advantageous surface permeability. It is well known that hollow micro-/nanostructures commonly endow the electrode materials with highly efficient electrochemical properties due to the existence of nanometer-sized building blocks and micrometer-sized assemblies [31]. The former possess a large electrode/electrolyte contact surface to offer efficient electroactive sites for reversible redox reactions, leading to a high specific capacity, and the latter show ideal mechanical properties and superior structural stability, resulting in an enhanced cycle life. To date, the physicochemical properties of hollow micro-/nanostructures can be tailored by changing the external shape, shell structure, internal architecture, size and chemical composition [32]. Especially noteworthy is that the tunable size of hollow structure can affect the SSA and ion transport paths. Once the size of hollow micro-/nanospheres is reduced, they will obtain more active sites and shorter ion diffusion paths compared with their large-size counterparts, thereby resulting in the improvement of specific capacity and rate performance [33].

Considering the above-mentioned analyses, herein, the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres were rationally constructed through a facile hydrothermal approach utilizing bifunctional CQDs as size regulators and conductive agents for the first time. By comparison to the pure Ni-Co-Se electrode, the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrode demonstrates enhanced electrochemical performance with greater specific capacity (151.1 mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1), better rate property (105.9 and 84.7 mAh g−1 at 50 and 100 A g−1, respectively) and longer cycling life (115.0 % of initial capacity retention at 10 A g−1 after 5000 cycles). Furthermore, a N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se//porous carbon (PC) HSC was assembled, displaying reasonable energy density (41.1 Wh kg−1 at 191.5 W kg−1), ultrahigh power density (38.3 kW kg−1 at 23.1 Wh kg−1) and superb cycling performance (95.7 % of initial capacity retention at 10 A g−1 after 5000 cycles). Additionally, in-depth electrochemical kinetic analyses utilizing electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests were executed to discuss the fundamental cause for the promoted electrochemical kinetics of N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se. More notably, the density functional theory (DFT) calculations reveal that introducing bifunctional N-CQDs can effectively change the electron redistribution, resulting in faster kinetics and enhanced electrochemical performance. These encouraging results show immense potential for battery-type electrode materials with ultrafast electrochemical kinetics in practical applications and give a valuable guideline for the rational design of ultrahigh power density energy storage devices.

Without additional treatment, all the chemical reagents that belong to analytical grade were employed. Ketjen black (KB) was provided by Shenzhen Teensky Tech Co., ltd. PC (7440–44-0, hole size ～2 nm) was provided by Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., ltd. Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, HNO3, H2SO4, anhydrous Na2CO3, Na2SeO3 and Ethylenediamine (EN) were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Shanghai Co. ltd.

The N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite was obtained via a one-step hydrothermal approach. Firstly, CQDs were obtained by an improved chemical oxidation method using KB as precursor and the detailed synthetic process of CQDs was demonstrated in our previous study [28]. In a typical synthetic route, 0.1021 g CQDs powders were dissolved in 50 mL H2O, and 0.4725 g Ni (NO3)2·6H2O and 0.4729 g Co(NO3)2·6H2O were further dissolved in the above solution and mixed for 15 min. Subsequently, 3.3 mL EN was poured into the aforementioned mixture and continually agitated for 15 min. Whereafter, 1.1241 g Na2SeO3 was put into the aforementioned solution with strong agitation for 20 min. After that, the resultant mixture was subjected to a hydrothermal autoclave (100 mL) lined with PTFE and maintained at 180 ℃ for 24 h. Ultimately, the products were gained by centrifuging and drying overnight at 50 ℃. For conducting a contrast, the pristine Ni-Co-Se was acquired via the similar approach but without employing CQDs in this synthetic process.

XRD (Rigaku D / max 2550 VB+ X-ray diffractometer), Raman spectrometer (renishaw inVia) and XPS (Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi+) were used to characterize the crystalline structures, phase component and chemical valence states of the as-prepared samples. The content of each component of the as-synthesized samples was executed on TGA (NETZSCH STA 449F5). The microstructures and morphologies of the resultant samples were detected by FESEM (Signa500) with corresponding EDS and elemental mappings and TEM/HRTEM (Titan G2 60–300).

The electrochemical measurements were executed at ambient temperature in the three- and two-electrode system with an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E). Before performing the electrochemical test, the working electrode was first obtained. Firstly, the active material, acetylene black and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) were subjected to mixing with a mass ratio of 7:2:1 in a certain amount of alcohol for the purpose of obtaining a uniformly distributed suspension. Afterwards, the suspension was dropped into a clean nickel foam collector and subsequently dried in a vacuum oven for 12 h. The mass loadings of the electroactive material on Ni foam were about 1.5 mg cm−2. A conventional three-electrode cell with Hg/HgO electrode, platinum electrode and 2 M KOH solution as reference electrode, counter electrode and electrolyte, respectively, was employed to evaluate the capacitive properties of the single electrode. In the two-electrode configuration, the HSC device was constructed by employing the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite as cathode, PC as anode and 2 M KOH solution as electrolyte. The mass ratio of the selected cathode and anode materials is 0.475, which can be balanced by the formula below:

In which C means specific capacitance, t relates to discharge time and ΔV signifies voltage window of the HSC device.

In this study, the properties of electronic structure were calculated using the first-principles DFT [34,35]. The Perdew-Burke-Ernzerh of functional [35] was used for exchange and correlation potentials, and only the Γ point was taken for the Brillouin-zone integration. The plane-wave cutoff energy was set as 500 eV for self-consistent field calculations. The convergence was reached when the residual forces on each atom were less than 0.02 eV/Å and the change of total energy was less than 10-6 eV. Grimme's DFT-D3 correction method [36] was used for van der Waals corrections. The figures of atomic structure were drawn by VESTA [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The formation process of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres composite
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 displays the formation procedure of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres composite. Firstly, the CQDs were obtained by an improved chemical oxidation approach with the KB as precursor. In particular, rich oxygen-containing functional groups in CQDs are extremely favorable for the construction of composites. In addition, EN and Na2SeO3 are critical factors to ensure the formation of N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se hollow microsphere composite. For EN, on one hand, the –NH2 functional group of EN can be used as a chelating agent. At the beginning, EN presents a strong tendency to chelate Ni and Co ions, further forming a stable Ni-Co-EN chelating complex. On the other hand, EN can serve as N source to ensure the formation of N-CQDs. The doped N atoms can efficiently enhance the electronic conductivity and pseudocapacitive property of CQDs. As for Na2SeO3, it not only provides Se source for the synthesis of products, but also affects the formation of hollow microspheres in the reaction process. At the beginning of the reaction, Na2SeO3 dissolved in H2O may be reduced by EN or Co2+, and Se cores may be rapidly formed [38,39]. Subsequently, the as-formed N-CQDs and Ni-Co-EN were attached to the Se core surface and further reacted to generate N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se particles. As the reaction temperature increases, the interdiffusion process continues, the Se core gradually disappears, and the internal cavity becomes obvious and complete, resulting in the formation of N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres. Besides, free Se2− in solution binds to atoms on the growing Se core through van der Waals forces during the synthesis process. Because of the feeble van der Waals forces, Se2− is absorbed on the surface of Se cores and pulled back into solution through the large electrostatic attraction between Se2− and δ+ in H2O, resulting in a rough nanoparticle surface of hollow microspheres [40,41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical properties of the pristine Ni-Co-Se and N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite
ce:section.ce:para: XRD test was executed to explore the composition, crystallinity and phase purity of the pure Ni-Co-Se and N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite. In the XRD patterns of the Ni-Co-Se and N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se samples, the typical diffraction peaks are well indexed to the cubic NiSe2 (PDF no.03–065-1843) and orthorhombic CoSe2 (PDF no.00–53-0449), as exhibited in Fig. 2 a. It is apparent that there are no additional peaks in the XRD patterns for the pristine Ni-Co-Se and N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite, implying that the synthesized products are quite pure. Note that no peak of CQDs can be noticed in the XRD pattern of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite, which may be due to the low content and crystallinity of CQDs. Raman spectra were utilized to further study the structural feature of the pure Ni-Co-Se and N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite. As exhibited in Fig. 2b, in the lower wave number region (100 ∼ 400 cm−1), the sharp peaks situated at 152 cm−1, 209 cm−1 and 237 cm−1 are associated with the Tg, Ag and Tg modes of NiSe2 [42], respectively, and the peak concentrated at 178 cm−1 is associated with the Se-Se stretching pattern of CoSe2 [43]. Compared with the spectrum of the pristine Ni-Co-Se, it can be discovered that the spectrum of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite presents two obvious peaks emerged at 1366 cm−1 and 1596 cm−1, which are well assigned to the d-band and G-band of CQDs, respectively, indicating that the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite was successfully prepared.

XPS was utilized to study the elemental composition, chemical state and bonding information of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite. Fig. S1 depicts the survey spectrum of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite. It is clear that all peaks can be attributed to Ni, Co, Se, C, O and N elements. The high-resolution spectra of all elements are shown in Fig. 2c-h, respectively. With respect to the Ni 2p XPS spectrum, the binding energies at 853.3 eV and 855.6 eV are ascribed to Ni2+ and Ni3+ of Ni 2p3/2 while 870.4 eV and 872.8 eV are ascribed to Ni2+ and Ni3+ of Ni 2p1/2, respectively [44,45]. Likewise, the peaks situated at 778.4 eV and 780.7 eV are well fitted to Co3+ and Co2+ of Co 2p3/2 and the peaks emerged at 793.5 eV and 796.8 eV are also well fitted to Co3+ and Co2+ of Co 2p1/2, respectively [46,47]. According to earlier studies [48], the emergence of trivalent state can be attributed to the partial oxidation or low coordination of Co ions on the surface or adjacent surface. The binding energies at 54.5 eV, 55.4 eV and 58.9 eV are attributable to Se 3d5/2, Se 3d3/2 and SeOx, respectively. Thereinto, the peak at 55.4 eV is indexed to the typical peak of metal-selenium bond [49], and the weak peak at 58.9 eV is considered to be Se-O bond on the surface, which is associated with the high oxidation state of Se on the surface. As exhibited in Fig. 2f, the C 1 s spectrum is classified into four components appeared at 284.8 eV, 285.4 eV, 286.6 eV and 288.2 eV, which are associated with CC, CN, CO and CO bonds, respectively [50]. The formation of CN bond implies that N atoms are efficiently doped into CQDs. From Fig. 2g, the XPS spectrum of N 1 s is divided into three fitted peaks appeared at 398.8 eV, 399.8 eV and 401.2 eV, which are well fitted to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N [51,52], respectively. Pyridinic N and pyrrolic N can undermine the carbon backbone of CC bond, which leads to the formation of defects in CQDs, thereby improving the specific capacitance of CQDs due to the contribution of pseudocapacitance [53–55]. For graphitic N, it is a special type of N atom that actually replaces carbon atoms in the carbon skeleton, boosting the electrical conductivity and thus the rate performance of CQDs [56,57]. As manifested in Fig. 2h, the peaks of O 1 s spectrum at 530.6 eV, 351.1 eV and 532.7 eV are indexed to CO, CO and COH/COC, respectively [58,59]. Based on the above results, XPS analyses demonstrate the co-existence of Ni, Co, Se, C, N and O elements in the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite. In addition, the TG analysis was carried out in air atmosphere from 20 ℃ to 900 ℃ at the rate of 10 ℃ min−1, as presented in Fig. 2i. Based on calculation and analysis, the mass loading of CQDs in the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite is about 19.35 wt%, further confirming the successful synthesis of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite.

In order to study the morphology and structure of the as-synthesized products, FESEM, TEM, HRTEM, EDS and element mapping were conducted, as exhibited in Fig. 3 . Obviously, the two products possess pretty similar morphology and structure, as demonstrated in Fig. 3a-d. The magnified FESEM image (Fig. 3d) shows that the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite still retains spherical feature with an opening pore, indicating that the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite possesses hollow microsphere structure composed of nanoparticles. Note that the size of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres is about 1–2 μm after CQDs doping into host matrix, demonstrating that the radius has been significantly reduced in comparison with the pristine Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres with a size of about 3.5–5 μm. It is suggested that the bifunctional CQDs is the key factor leading to the size variation of hollow microspheres. Fig. 3e shows the TEM image of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres, further illustrating that the hollow microsphere structure can be validated by internal white area and edge black area, which is in line with that observed from FESEM images. Notably, three lattice fringes can be clearly distinguished from HRTEM images in Fig. 3f,g. The lattice fringes of 0.30 nm, 0.19 nm and 0.204 nm correspond to the characteristic crystal plane (200) of NiSe2, (211) of CoSe2 and (101) of CQDs, respectively. More importantly, the Ni, Co, Se, C, N and O elements are dispersed evenly in elemental mapping images as demonstrated in Fig. 3i, which is in line with the XPS result. As expected, the atomic ratio of Ni, Co and Se in the EDS analysis (Fig. 3h and Table S1) is around 1:1:4, which is in line with the XRD test result, demonstrating that the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres with the same ratio of NiSe2 and CoSe2 particles have been successfully synthesized. Hence, the molecular formula of Ni-Co-Se can be expressed as NiCoSe4. All of the above characterizations and analyses can serve as the evidence for the successful synthesis of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance and kinetics analyses of the pristine Ni-Co-Se and N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: According to the systematic physiochemical characterizations and analyses above, it can be concluded that the resultant N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite possesses structural and compositional merits, such as great electrode/electrolyte contact area, short electrolyte ion diffusion path, high conductivity and good structural stability. To analyze the electrochemical properties of these prepared samples in detail, a standard three-electrode configuration was used to estimate the supercapacitive properties of the pristine Ni-Co-Se and N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrodes utilizing 2 M KOH as the electrolyte. Fig. 4 a exhibits the characteristic CV profiles of the pure Ni-Co-Se and N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrodes at 5 mV s−1 with a potential range of 0–0.6 V. Apparently, the CV profiles both present a pair of strong and broad redox peaks, implying a typical combination of double‑layer capacitance and Faradaic battery behavior. The former originates from the adsorption and desorption of OH–, and the latter derives from reversible redox reactions between Ni2+/Ni3+, Co2+/Co3+ and Co3+/Co4+ [60,17]. Furthermore, the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se electrode has a larger integral area of CV profile than that of the Ni-Co-Se electrode, indicating a higher specific capacity.

In order to conduct a striking contrast, Fig. 4b gives the galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) profiles of every electrode at 1 A g−1 in the potential window of 0–0.5 V. All GCD profiles present distinct charge/discharge plateaus that conform to the CV results, further elucidating the Faradaic redox characteristic of the pristine Ni-Co-Se and N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrodes. Notably, the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrode exhibits a larger integral area in the discharge region compared with the pristine Ni-Co-Se electrode, indicating the better charge storage performance, which is attributed to the role of CQDs in regulating size. Due to the introduction of CQDs, the size of the as-formed hollow microspheres composite becomes smaller, leading to an enlarged SSA, which can be verified by the N2 adsorption–desorption test (Fig. S2), thereby favoring the exposure of more electroactive sites and thus increasing the specific capacity. The GCD curves of the Ni-Co-Se and N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrodes at diverse current densities are exhibited in Fig. 4c and Fig. 4d, respectively. The specific capacities of these as-fabricated electrodes are calculated from the GCD profiles at current densities of 1–100 A g−1, as plotted in Fig. 4e. Significantly, the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrode possesses higher specific capacities of 151.1, 150.6, 149.8, 146.4, 138.9, 128.8, 119.1, 105.9 and 84.7 mAh g−1 at diverse current densities of 1, 2, 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 50 and 100 A g−1, respectively, which are better than those of the pristine Ni-Co-Se electrode (128.6, 127.5, 125.6, 121.5, 111.7, 100.0, 91.3, 78.9 and 59.7 mAh g−1 at 1, 2, 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 50 and 100 A g−1, respectively). Notably, when the current density rises 50-fold from 1 A g−1 to 50 A g−1, the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrode presents an ultrahigh capacity retention of 70.1 %, and the capacity retention still can reach up to 56.1 % even at 100 A g−1, which are superior to those of the pristine Ni-Co-Se electrode (61.3 and 46.4 % capacity retention at 50 and 100 A g−1, respectively). The boosted specific capacity and rate property of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrode can be correlated with the introduction of bifunctional N-CQDs. On one hand, N-CQDs as size regulators can reduce the size of hollow microspheres, which leads to the enlarged SSA and shortened ion transport channel, thereby enhancing the specific capacity and rate property. On the other hand, N-CQDs as conductive agents can boost the electrical conductivity of Ni-Co-Se, which results in the rapid electron transport, thereby enhancing the rate capability. To the best of our knowledge, the specific capacity and rate capability of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite are closed to or even better than those of selenide-based electrode materials reported in previous research, as shown in Table S3. Additionally, the cycle stability of the as-fabricated Ni-Co-Se and N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrodes was estimated by repeated charge/discharge measurements after 5000 cycles at 10 A g−1, as exhibited in Fig. 4f. As expected, the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se electrode manifests the enhanced cycle performance with 115.0 % of initial capacity retention after 5000 cycles at 10 A g−1 compared to the pristine Ni-Co-Se electrode (108.1 %), and the coulombic efficiency floats around 100 %. The superior cycle stability of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se electrode can be verified by the minor morphological change of the FESEM image and little change of the XRD pattern after 5000 cycles (Fig. S3), which is probably due to the synergism of the unique hollow composite structure and the improved binding strength between N-CQDs and Ni-Co-Se [61].

To illustrate the enhanced electrochemical kinetics of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrode, EIS measurement was first executed at open circuit potential with the varying frequencies range of 0.01–100 kHz. Fig. 5 a exhibits the Nyquist plots of the Ni-Co-Se and N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrodes. It is clear that the EIS curves of each electrode consist of intercepts and semicircles in the high-frequency area as well as oblique lines in the low-frequency area. Furthermore, the enlarged view of EIS spectra, EIS fitting curves and impedance equivalent circuit diagram are displayed in the inset of Fig. 5a. Interestingly, the estimated internal resistance (R s) and charge transfer resistance (R ct) of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrode are 0.62 Ω and 0.90 Ω (Table S2), respectively, which are lower than those of the pristine Ni-Co-Se electrode (0.74 Ω and 1.60 Ω, respectively), indicating that the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se electrode has lower resistance and quicker charge transfer kinetics than those of the Ni-Co-Se electrode. Additionally, the Nyquist plots show an oblique line in the low-frequency area, which relates to the capacitive performance. The oblique line of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrode is closer to the y axis compared with that of the Ni-Co-Se electrode, illustrating that the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrode possesses a heightened capacitive behavior, which can well explain why the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrode can acquire remarkably enhanced rate property and cycling stability. Fig. 5b illustrates that the phase angles of the as-fabricated electrodes are 75.6° and 77.9°, respectively. Note that the phase angle of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrode is greater than that of the pristine Ni-Co-Se electrode, which demonstrates a superior capacitive behavior [62]. This result is in accordance with the EIS analyses. Fig. 5c exhibits the evolution of C“(ω) versus frequency. The operating frequency (
f 0
) is related to the relaxation time constant τ0 (τ0 =
1 /
f 0
, the minimum time required for an efficiency of greater than 50 %), and they are the significant quantitative indicators of reversible charge and discharge rate of the electrode [63]. As illustrated in Fig. 5c, each curve shows a sharp peak that corresponds to the maximum capacitance C”(ω) with the decrease of frequency, which denotes a transition point from pure resistance behavior to pure capacitance behavior [64]. Notably, the τ0 of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrode is 2.15 s, which is shorter than that of the pristine Ni-Co-Se electrode (4.64 s), further demonstrating that the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrode possesses the significantly faster ions diffusion, and thus contributes to the boosted charge transfer kinetics and rate property. Furthermore, based on the plots in the Warburg region, the ion diffusion coefficient (D) of the as-fabricated electrodes can be quantitatively calculated by the subsequent equations [65,66]:

In which R represents gas constant, T stands for experiment temperature, F denotes Faradic constant, A is absolute area, n represents the number of electrons per molecule participating the electronic transfer reaction, C stands for the concentration of OH—, σ is Warburg factor and ω denotes angular frequency. The linear correlation between |Z| and ω-
1/2 is demonstrated in Fig. 5d, and the σ value is the slope of the fitted line. The calculated D value of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se electrode is 2.2 × 10-12 cm2 s−1, which is remarkably higher than that of the Ni-Co-Se electrode (1.2 × 10-12 cm2 s−1), indicating that the introduction of N-CQDs is able to decrease the size of the hollow microspheres, which leads to shortened and unimpeded pathways for effective ion diffusion, thus resulting in excellent ion diffusion kinetics.

To further probe into the in-depth electrochemical kinetics of the as-synthesized pristine Ni-Co-Se and N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrodes, a series of semi-quantitative analyses based on CV curves were carried out. Fig. 5e,f reveal the CV curves of the pristine Ni-Co-Se and N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrodes at varying sweep rates from 2 mV s−1 to 100 mV s−1. It is found that the shapes of all CV profiles are nearly identical. Meanwhile, the oxidation and reduction peaks shift slightly to higher and lower potentials, signifying that the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se electrode possesses excellent reversibility, ideal conductivity and outstanding rate performance. The OH— diffusion coefficient (D) is closely related to linear correlation between the peak currents (Ip ) and the square of sweep rate (
v

), as demonstrated in Fig. 5g,h. The D of OH— is calculated by the Randles-Sevcik equation [67]:

In which n is the electrons number in the transfer process, A denotes electrode area and

stands for OH— concentration. The fitting slope values of the pristine Ni-Co-Se electrode are 23.64 and −16.31, and those of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se electrode are 29.79 and −21.79. Therefore, the calculated D values of Ni-Co-Se are about 1.79 × 10-11 cm2 s−1 and 8.42 × 10-12 cm2 s−1 while those of N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se are around 2.81 × 10-11 cm2 s−1 and 2.37 × 10-11 cm2 s−1. Whereas, it is noteworthy that the average D value of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrode (2.59 × 10-11 cm2 s−1) is greater than that of the pristine Ni-Co-Se electrode (1.31 × 10-11 cm2 s−1), further verifying the fast ion diffusion of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrode, which results from the reduced-size hollow microspheres with shortened diffusion channel. Furthermore, the electrochemical properties, which are mainly controlled by diffusion or capacitive behavior, can be quantitatively determined by the equation below [68]:

In which i is the current under a fixed voltage ( V ),

are respectively related to the contributions from the surface capacitive and diffusion-controlled charges. Thus, based on

at specific potentials, it is easy to quantify the fraction of the current according to the surface capacitive and diffusion-controlled contributions. As exhibited in Fig. 5k, the capacity contribution of the Ni-Co-Se electrode increases from 61.51 % to 92.86 % as the current density rises from 2 to 100 A g−1, obviously illustrating that the surface capacitive effect dominates the total capacity. The contribution of the capacitive effect in the total capacity of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite electrode is further boosted at all current densities, which contributes to the rapid redox reactions and fast charge transfer during the electrochemical process. This can be attributed to the participation of CQDs with electric double-layer capacitance feature and doped N atoms with typical pseudocapacitance characteristic. This is in line with the above analysis results obtained from the EIS test.

To further understand the role of bifunctional N-CQDs on the reaction kinetics, DFT calculations were used to explore the interfacial charge behavior of N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se. Fig. 6 a,b respectively exhibit the front view and top view of optimized N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se structure. In order to understand the charge behavior of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se interface, the charge density difference at the interface of N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se was investigated. Considering the random nature of nitrogen atom doping, we select the case of nitrogen atom doping in the carbon layer region on the CoSe2 side for the charge density difference analysis. According to Fig. 6c, the plane-averaged differential charge density curve shows a significant oscillation at the interface position, which undoubtedly proves that the electrons migrate from N-CQDs to Ni-Co-Se because of the strong polarization between the interfaces [69]. The charge density difference further reveals the charge redistribution, as displayed in the inset of Fig. 6c. Apparently, electrons deplete around the N-CQDs (blue) and accumulate (pink) around the Co atoms, further demonstrating that the introduction of N-CQDs can induce significant charge transfer and redistribution between N-CQDs and Ni-Co-Se, which leads to electron transfer from N-CQDs to Ni-Co-Se. Moreover, the analysis of the work function of N-CQDs and Ni-Co-Se can shed more light on the origin and direction of charge transfer at the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se interface. As exhibited in Fig. 6d,e, it can be clearly seen that the work function of N-CQDs is 4.26 eV, which is lower than that of Ni-Co-Se (4.68 eV). When the two materials are in contact, spontaneous electrons transfer will flow from N-CQDs to Ni-Co-Se driven by the difference of work function [70]. Therefore, Ni-Co-Se gains more electrons and N-CQDs are filled with more positive charges (holes), resulting in the presence of internal electric field. The hole accumulation at N-CQDs can make the N-CQDs side strongly attract OH– on the phase boundaries, which contributes to faster reaction kinetics. Furthermore, when electrons deviate from the nucleus, the constraint of electrons on the nucleus is weakened and the migration rate of electrons is notably improved [71]. The above results illustrate that introducing bifunctional N-CQDs can cause the charge redistribution, thereby enhancing the electron mobility and reaction kinetics of N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se.

In view of the aforementioned results, the excellent capacitive properties of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite, especially the extraordinary rate property, are mainly attributed to these dimensions below: (1) The bifunctional CQDs are served as size regulators to induce the formation of smaller-size N-CQDs decorated nanoparticles-assembled Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres composite. The smaller-size N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres possess a greater SSA for more redox electroactive sites and shorter electrolyte ion transport paths, thereby causing an enhancement of specific capacity and rate capability. (2) The bifunctional N-CQDs as conductive agents can decrease the R ct at the interface and construct high-conductivity networks in the micro/nano-scaled regions, contributing to the fast charge transport and thus enhancing the reaction kinetics. (3) The unusual hollow composite structure possesses a huge surface area, good mechanical characteristic and structural stability. The internal hollow structure not merely enlarges the contact area between electrolyte and electroactive material and facilitates the permeation of electrolyte, providing more active sites for highly reversible redox processes, but also shortens the ion diffusion paths, resulting in outstanding specific capacity and enhanced reaction kinetics. In particular, the splendid structural stability accommodates the volume expansion during charge and discharge processes, leading to an ideal cycle stability. (4) Due to the synergism of high-conductivity N-CQDs and high-capacity and stable Ni-Co-Se, the as-synthesized N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres composite presents high specific capacity, exceptional rate property and long cycling life.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se//PC HSC device
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the potential of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite for practical applications, a HSC device was assembled by employing N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se as the cathode and PC as the anode in 2 M KOH electrolyte, as exhibited in Fig. 7 a. The CV and GCD curves of PC are shown in Fig. S4. Owing to the different potential windows of N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se (0 ∼ 0.5 V) and PC (–1 ∼ 0 V), the voltage of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se//PC HSC device can be broadened to 1.5 V, as demonstrated by the CV curves in Fig. S5. Fig. 7b exhibits the CV profiles of the as‑fabricated N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se//PC HSC device at sweep rates ranging from 2 mV s−1 to 200 mV s−1. Apparently, there is no apparent distortion for the CV profiles at all sweep rates, revealing the excellent reversibility and prominent rate capability. Moreover, the shapes of CV curves all depart from the rectangular shape due to the battery-type charge storage mechanism of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se cathode. The GCD profiles and the calculated specific capacitances of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se//PC HSC device at varying current densities from 1 A g−1 to 200 A g−1 are exhibited in Fig. 7c and Fig. 7d, respectively. It is found that the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se//PC HSC device achieves a specific capacitance of 131.6F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and still remains 62.5 % of the value even at a superhigh current density of 200 A g−1. Fig. 7e shows the Ragone plot of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se//PC HSC device. Based on the calculation of the GCD profiles, the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se//PC HSC device possesses a reasonable energy density of 41.1 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 191.5 W kg−1 and still remains a large value of 23.1 Wh kg−1 at an extremely high power density of 38.3 kW kg−1. The energy density of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se//PC HSC device under high power density is larger in contrast with that of the previously reported selenide-based HSC devices, such as H-NiCoSe2//AC (25.5 Wh kg−1 at 3.8 kW kg−1) [60], Ni0.5Co0.5Se2//RGO (15.1 Wh kg−1 at 22.2 kW kg−1) [72], (NiCo)9Se8/(NiCo)0.85Se//AC (22.5 Wh kg−1 at 7.5 kW kg−1) [73], (Ni,Co)Se2/NiCo-LDH//PC (24.0 Wh kg−1 at 8.3 kW kg−1) [74], NiCo2Se4//AC (25.0 Wh kg−1 at 0.5 kW kg−1) [75], (Ni0.33Co0.67)Se2//AC (23.0 Wh kg−1 at 6.4 kW kg−1) [76], NiSe2/CoSe2//N,S-rGO (22.7 Wh kg−1 at 26.1 kW kg−1) [17], Ni0.8Co0.2Se2//AC (31.3 Wh kg−1 at 4.3 kW kg−1) [77], NiCo-LDH/(NiCo)Se2@CC//AC (30.7 Wh kg−1 at 8 kW kg−1) [78] and so on. The cyclic stability of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se//PC HSC device was also examined by repeated charge and discharge measurements at 10 A g−1 after 5000 cycles, as demonstrated in Fig. 7f. After 5000 charging/discharging cycles, the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se//PC HSC reveals a 95.7 % of capacity retention accompanied with an outstanding electrochemical coulombic efficiency (nearly 100 %), illustrating splendid electrochemical stability and good reversibility. In consequence, the rational design and pairing of the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite cathode with ultrahigh rate property and the PC anode with exceptional capacitive behavior endow the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se//PC HSC with an ability to remain a high energy density at an ultrahigh power density, which makes the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se composite become an appropriate choice for simultaneously achieving high energy/power density HSCs.

In summary, the bifunctional CQDs are first obtained by a revised chemical oxidation method using KB as precursor, and further utilized as size regulators and conductive agents to prepare new N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres composite through a facile hydrothermal method. When for the first time employed as the cathode for HSC device, the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se hollow spheres composite exhibits a satisfactory specific capacity of 151.1 mAh g−1 at 1 A/g, and still remains a large specific capacity of 84.7 mAh g−1 even at 100 A/g. The prominent rate property can be ascribed to the introduction of bifunctional N-CQDs in composite, where N-CQDs as conductive agents contribute to the rapid electron transfer, and N-CQDs as size regulators shorten the ion transport distance and speed up ion transport rate. In-depth electrochemical kinetic analyses and DFT calculations confirm that the introduction of bifunctional N-CQDs is a key factor to achieve ultrafast electrochemical reaction kinetics. Furthermore, a novel HSC device is constructed based on the N-CQDs/Ni-Co-Se hollow microspheres composite cathode with ultrafast electrochemical kinetics, achieving a maximum energy density of 41.1 Wh kg−1 at 191.5 W kg−1 and an extremely high power density of 38.3 kW kg−1 at 23.1 Wh kg−1. This impressive study offers a new perspective for the preparation of ultrahigh rate battery-type electrode materials with adjustable size and composition to simultaneously obtain high energy and power density HSCs.
